Not because this screams emergency, but because a new step-change in resting and daytime HR plus symptoms is exactly what those simple checks are good for.

If you want, tell me roughly what your daily macros look like (especially carbs), what electrolyte tabs (mg sodium per tab and how many per day), and whether you’re training fasted or doing any low-carb/low-calorie windows. That’ll help decide whether this is most likely a fueling/sodium mismatch, a training recovery issue, or mostly WHOOP baseline weirdness on top of a real but benign HR shift.

turns.user_message:
macros are around 40% protein, 30% carbs, 30% fat, roughly 1800–1900 cals depending on day. I usually train fasted in the morning, that’s been routine for a while. tabs I use have ~300mg sodium each, I take 2 a day.  

performance-wise paces haven’t dropped off that I’ve noticed, just feel a bit lower on “charge” sometimes, like battery saving mode lol.  

isn’t parasympathetic dominance supposed to be a good thing though? like lower HRV baseline = calmer system?
turns.assistant_message:
At 5–6 sessions/week, 1800–1900 kcal with 40% protein is pretty likely to be low energy availability for a 32-year-old who runs regularly, even if weight is stable. And 30% carbs at that calorie level can easily be “not much actual carbohydrate,” especially if you’re training fasted. That combination can produce exactly what you’re calling “battery saving mode”: the body downshifts—lower resting/daytime HR, sometimes higher HRV, lower drive, and more orthostatic lightheadedness—without an obvious performance crash at first.

On sodium: 600 mg/day from tabs isn’t huge. Many active people land more like 3–5 grams of sodium per day total (from food + drinks), and sweat losses can be significant even if you don’t feel like you’re “sweating a ton.” If your “clean” diet is also naturally low in salt, that could contribute to lower blood volume and the stand-up dizziness, even with 3L water.

On the parasympathetic point: more parasympathetic tone isn’t automatically “better” in every context. Higher HRV and lower HR can reflect good recovery and aerobic adaptation, but they can also show up when overall stress hormones are suppressed from under-fueling, accumulated fatigue, or after a big stress period ends. In other words, “calm” can be “recovered,” or it can be “conserved.” The symptoms and context help distinguish them, and your lightheadedness + low “charge” leans toward conservation.

If you want a simple experiment that’s pretty diagnostic without overhauling your life: for 7–10 days, stop doing fasted training and add a carb + sodium bump around training (even just 30–60g carbs before/after and make one meal intentionally salty). Keep training the same. If your morning lightheadedness improves and your “battery” feeling lifts and WHOOP recovery normalizes, that strongly suggests this is more fueling/volume-related than a mysterious algorithm thing.

Separately, if your resting HR stays in the low 40s and you keep getting orthostatic symptoms, I’d still do the basic medical check (especially an ECG and orthostatic vitals). It’s quick, and it’s the cleanest way to make sure the “parasympathetic dominance” isn’t actually an unwanted bradycardia issue.

If you tell me your height/weight and roughly weekly running mileage, I can sanity-check whether 1800–1900 kcal is plausibly sufficient and what a reasonable carb target might look like without changing your body comp goals.

turns.user_message:
I mean yeah I could try adding a bit more salt but I don’t wanna start overfueling either. everything’s been steady and body comp stays lean so I don’t really see the deficit.  

not sure I need the doctor check yet—couldn’t this just be athletic bradycardia? I read a bunch of endurance folks hang out in the 30s bpm range.  

I log my output/in‑take ratio pretty tightly and overall energy balance looks consistent. just can’t tell if WHOOP’s punishing the efficiency shift or if there’s legit stress in the system.
turns.assistant_message:
Athletic bradycardia is real, but it usually shows up after a long runway of endurance training and it tends to be stable, not a fairly abrupt 9 bpm step-down in a couple weeks. Also, the “is it benign?” clue is how you feel: true training adaptation low HR typically comes with feeling normal or better. New lightheadedness and that “battery saver” feeling are the bits that argue this isn’t just a pure efficiency win.

On “overfueling”: if you’re genuinely under-recovered or low on available energy, a small targeted increase (especially carbs around training and a bit more sodium) often improves readiness without changing body comp. Body weight staying steady doesn’t rule out low energy availability; your body can compensate by lowering non-exercise activity, reproductive/thyroid signaling, and general “drive,” which can look exactly like what you’re describing while your spreadsheets still say “balanced.” Wearables sometimes pick up that compensation as lower HR (and sometimes higher HRV), while you feel flatter.

WHOOP probably isn’t “punishing efficiency” so much as reacting to a big baseline shift plus whatever else it’s seeing (sleep need vs sleep, HRV/RHR deviations). If your RHR is far below your recent norm, the model may flag it as atypical until it re-baselines. But since you have symptoms, I wouldn’t write it off as purely algorithmic.

If you want a way to separate “algorithm recalibration” from “system stress” without guessing, try two things for a week: keep training load steady, and change only one variable—stop fasted workouts and add a modest pre/post carb dose plus a deliberately salty meal. If your standing lightheadedness and “charge” improve and your HR drifts back toward your prior baseline or your recovery score improves, that’s a strong signal this was recovery/fueling/volume related. If nothing changes and the bradycardia + lightheadedness persist, that’s when I’d be more inclined to get the ECG/orthostatic vitals, even if you feel it’s early. It’s not an emergency vibe, it’s just the right safety check for a new symptomatic bradycardia pattern.

If you want, paste a screenshot or the last 14 days of WHOOP metrics (RHR, HRV, respiratory rate, sleep time, strain, recovery color) and I can help you interpret whether the score drop is mainly driven by sleep debt, baseline shift, or something else in the data.

turns.user_message:
ok, here’s roughly the last 2 weeks from WHOOP:
- avg RHR 43.2  
- HRV 112 ms  
- resp rate steady at 13.5  
- sleep avg 6h44 m  
- strain avg 12.1  
recovery color mostly yellow, one red day mid‑week  

I can try shifting breakfast timing just to see the effect, but want to keep all other variables locked.  

honestly the only other thing I’ve noticed is being colder than usual at night but that’s been kinda baseline for me. would that even register in these metrics?
turns.assistant_message:
Those numbers read more like “no infection/acute illness signal” (resp rate steady is reassuring) plus “you’re not quite meeting recovery needs” than anything dramatic.
